utterances: Yes.
He, number one, is a director.
One of his pictures did win Best Picture - it was ""Rebecca."" But that award went to the producer, David O.
Selznick.
Anywhere, here's a couple of others: ""Breakfast at Tiffany's,"" ""The Dirty Dozen,"" ""Last Tango in Paris,"" ""Serpico,"" ""Rebel Without a Cause.
Bob, I'm shocked.
Yeah.
None of those movies were nominated for Best Picture?
Not one.
I think it's really egregious.
You would think that something fabulous would've bumped them out.
The Dirty Dozen"" was bumped by Rex Harrison riding a pink snail in ""Dr.
Doolittle."" ""Last Tango in Paris"" and ""Serpico"" were bumped by a picture that nobody really remembers anymore called ""A Touch of Class.
Never heard of it.
Yeah.
It's like a nothing little comedy.
It sounds like a flower shop.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Yeah, well, that's fair.
But the most egregious example of all is that ""Oliver"" won for Best Picture in 1968.
That's a musical.
Yes, and it was a perfectly sweet musical but kind of okay.
And what was not nominated that year was ""2001: A Space Odyssey.
Wow.
Arguably one of the great pictures ever.
So, how is it that films that we now consider essential, classics, you know, get left out?
Well, frequently, in the case of something like ""Last Tango"" or ""Serpico"" or something like that, the Academy tends to shy away from things that are either overtly sexual or are really violent.
Which is why ""Last Tango in Paris"" wasn't nominated.
Films like ""Pulp Fiction"" even?
Yeah.
Because that was nominated.
That got nominated but it didn't win.
Right.
And I think those kinds of pictures have a problem just sort of generally, and that's because the folks who are voting for these tend to be a good deal older than the general public.
I mean, movies are made for 18 to 26-year-olds basically.
That's the prime audience.
The people voting for the Oscars include Mickey Rooney, Shirley Temple, Kirk Douglas.
I mean, this is not to say that they aren't forward thinking and fabulous and youthful at heart, but they're not the same as the audience.
And Bob you're mentioning, obviously, a lot of actors, and actors presumably make up a pretty big segment of the people who vote.
Yes, this is true.
There are many more actors than there are directors, and because they're the biggest chunk of the Academy, they also wield a lot of influence.
And they in fact tend to go for actor's pictures.
You'll see something like ""Kramer vs. Kramer"" gets a nod over ""Apocalypse Now,"" because ""Apocalypse Now"" is a director's picture.
It's Francis Ford Coppola showing off and it's fabulous and all that kind of thing.
But ""Kramer vs. Kramer"" is a sort of a domestic drama that shows off actors and actors get into that a lot.
And then ""Raging Bull,"" which is, again, considered a classic, lost to ""Ordinary People.
Exactly.
It's exactly the same kind of thing.
And ""Ordinary People"" is a good picture, right?
Yeah.
But it doesn't really hold up the way that ""Raging Bull"" does.
Raging Bull was regarded as one of the great pictures of that decade.
So if it favors actor's films, does this suggest that a movie like ""The Hurt Locker"" might win tomorrow night versus, you know, a sweeping huge director's film like ""Avatar""?
It does in fact suggest that.
Avatar"" is a big splashy picture.

But I tell you, if I were an actor and I were looking at ""Avatar,"" I would realize how unimportant I was to that, because me with a blue tail and nine feet tall and an elongated face, not quite the same as...
You'd get a lot of attention in the building.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Well, that's true.

DIONNE (Columnist, The Washington Post): Good to be with you.

If we're shipping more stuff overseas, that's employing people.
It's creating income and profits.
If we're importing less, that means that companies here that are competing with foreign producers are able to produce more, and that employs people as well.
Now, as to why this happened, for one anyone who's traveled to Europe this summer knows this, the dollar has been weaker against foreign currencies.
So that's helped us to export more goods and services.
Essentially, our stuff is cheaper on the world market, so people want to buy it.
It's also because our weakish economy.
We're not buying as much stuff from the rest of the world, so that's helped to shrink our imports.
But we are still buying a lot of foreign oil at super high prices, and so that keeps the trade deficit high.
And the trade deficit 56 billion dollars.
That is high.
Yeah.
It is a pretty big number.
Long term economists say that number is a detriment to our economic growth.

It may help the poor here to buy cheaper goods, but overall, if we're spending money on Chinese TVs and espresso machines, clothes, or Saudi Arabian oil, then big picture, there's less of our money to be spent on American-made products from American companies.
Since 2000, our trade deficit increased billion of dollars, and we've lost four million manufacturing jobs.
I spoke to Peter Morici about this.
He's an economist and business professor at the University of Maryland.
He says we have a long term structural deficit, and that forces us to borrow a lot of money.
We are borrowing from foreigners at a pace of about 50 billion dollars a month.
We owe the world about 6.5 trillion dollars, and that's one of the reasons that credit is drying up in the United States.
Foreigners don't want to loan us any more money.
